title: Setup Python environment and dependencies
description: Install all required packages for dataset generation, model inference, evaluation, and visualization. Use vLLM for efficient batched inference on the A6000 GPU.

steps.step1: Create a conda environment or use the system Python (3.10+). Install core packages: pip install vllm>=0.4.0 transformers>=4.40 torch>=2.1 datasets numpy pandas scipy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn tqdm jsonlines
steps.step2: Install additional utilities: pip install accelerate bitsandbytes sentencepiece protobuf
steps.step3: Verify GPU availability: run `python -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available(), torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))"` and confirm RTX A6000 is detected
steps.step4: Create the project directory structure: mkdir -p data/{seed_42,seed_123,seed_456} results/{raw,parsed,aggregated} figures logs
steps.step5: Download model weights in advance to avoid timeout during inference. Pre-download: microsoft/Phi-3.5-mini-instruct, meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, Qwen/Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct-AWQ

title: Generate FlipBench dataset (3 seeds)
description: Programmatically generate 1,200 matched forward-backward reasoning pairs across 4 domains (propositional logic, arithmetic, relational reasoning, function computation), each with 3 difficulty levels (100 pairs per level). Generate 3 independent dataset versions using seeds 42, 123, and 456 for statistical robustness. Every instance must have a verified ground-truth answer.

steps.step1: Implement the Propositional Logic generator (generate_logic.py): Generate rule sets over a vocabulary of 8-12 propositions (P, Q, R, S, ...). Difficulty 1: 1-hop (1 rule, 1 fact, derive conclusion). Difficulty 2: 2-hop (2 chained rules, 1 starting fact). Difficulty 3: 3-hop (3 chained rules plus 2-3 distractor rules). Forward: given rules + facts, determine if target proposition is True/False. Backward: given rules + target truth value, determine what initial fact(s) must hold. Ensure unique solutions by construction. Output format: JSON with fields {id, domain, difficulty, direction, problem_text, answer, matched_pair_id, rules, facts}.
steps.step2: Implement the Arithmetic Reasoning generator (generate_arithmetic.py): Generate word problems involving basic operations (+, -, *, /). Difficulty 1: single operation (e.g., compute 7*3+4). Difficulty 2: two chained operations. Difficulty 3: three chained operations with mixed operators. Forward: given all operands + operations, compute result. Backward: given result + all-but-one operand, find missing operand. Use integers only (range 1-100 for operands, ensure clean division). Verify unique solvability. Same JSON output format.
steps.step3: Implement the Relational/Causal Reasoning generator (generate_relational.py): Generate entity-relation graphs using family relationships (parent, child, sibling, grandparent, uncle/aunt, cousin). Difficulty 1: single-hop (A is B's parent, what is B to A?). Difficulty 2: two-hop transitive (A is B's parent, B is C's parent, what is A to C?). Difficulty 3: three-hop with 3-5 distractor entities. Forward: given relationships, derive target relation. Backward: given relationships + target relation, identify the entity. Use a name pool of 20 common names. Verify unique answers.
steps.step4: Implement the Function Computation generator (generate_functions.py): Generate simple Python functions using operations: multiply, add, subtract, modulo, integer divide. Difficulty 1: single operation (f(x) = a*x + b). Difficulty 2: two composed operations (f(x) = a*(x+b) + c). Difficulty 3: three composed operations with conditionals (if x > threshold: path_a else: path_b). Forward: given function + input, compute output. Backward: given function + output, find input. Ensure invertibility by restricting to bijective functions on the integer domain [1, 50]. Verify unique inverse.
steps.step5: Run all 4 generators for each of the 3 seeds (42, 123, 456). Save each seed's dataset as data/seed_{N}/flipbench.json. Each file should contain exactly 2,400 instances (1,200 pairs × 2 directions). Run verification: for every pair, confirm that the forward answer and backward answer are both correct by re-solving programmatically.
steps.step6: Compute and log dataset statistics: total instances per domain/difficulty/direction, answer distribution (check for class imbalance in True/False answers for logic), numeric answer range for arithmetic, unique entity counts for relational. Save to data/dataset_stats.json.
steps.step7: Create a random-guessing baseline: for each instance type, compute the expected accuracy of random guessing. Logic (binary True/False): 50%. Arithmetic (open-ended integer): ~0%. Relational (choose from N entities): 1/N. Function (open-ended integer): ~0%. Save baseline expectations to results/random_baseline.json.

title: Design evaluation prompts and answer parser
description: Create standardized prompt templates for each domain and a robust answer parser that extracts final answers from model outputs. The prompt must be identical for forward and backward variants (aside from the problem text itself) to ensure fair comparison.

steps.step1: Design prompt templates (prompts.py). Each prompt follows the format: System message setting the role ('You are a precise reasoning assistant. Answer the question below. Give your final answer on the last line in the format: ANSWER: <your answer>'), followed by the problem text. No few-shot examples (zero-shot evaluation). The template is domain-agnostic to avoid giving direction-specific hints.
steps.step2: Implement answer parser (parse_answers.py). Extract the text after 'ANSWER:' from model output. For logic: parse 'True'/'False' (case-insensitive, also accept 'Yes'/'No'). For arithmetic: parse integer (strip whitespace, handle negative). For relational: parse entity name (fuzzy match against valid entities). For function: parse integer. Log parsing failures separately.
steps.step3: Implement a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompt variant for ablation: same as above but add 'Think step by step before giving your final answer.' after the problem text. Save as a separate prompt template.
steps.step4: Test prompts + parser on 5 hand-crafted examples per domain to verify correctness before running full evaluation.

title: Evaluate simple baselines
description: Establish lower bounds with two simple baselines: (1) random guessing and (2) a regex/heuristic pattern-matching baseline that uses surface-level cues without reasoning.

steps.step1: Random baseline: for each instance, sample a random answer from the valid answer space. Logic: random choice from {True, False}. Arithmetic: random integer from [1, 100]. Relational: random choice from mentioned entities. Function: random integer from [1, 50]. Run with 3 seeds, compute mean accuracy per domain/direction/difficulty. Save to results/random_baseline_results.json.
steps.step2: Heuristic baseline: implement simple pattern-matching rules. Logic: always answer 'True' (majority class bias check). Arithmetic forward: attempt to evaluate any expression found in the text using regex + eval. Arithmetic backward: always guess the median of operand range. Relational: guess the first mentioned entity. Function forward: attempt to eval the function string. Function backward: always guess 25 (midpoint of [1,50]). Save to results/heuristic_baseline_results.json.
steps.step3: Compute baseline metrics: forward accuracy, backward accuracy, DRG, and consistency rate for both baselines across all domains and difficulty levels. These serve as sanity checks — any model should significantly outperform these.

title: Evaluate standard instruction-tuned models (strong baselines)
description: Evaluate standard (non-reasoning-optimized) instruction-tuned LLMs as strong baselines. These represent the current state-of-the-art for general-purpose models without specialized reasoning training.

steps.step1:
Evaluate Phi-3.5-mini-instruct (3.8B) on seed_42 dataset using vLLM. Load model with vLLM (LLM class, dtype=float16, gpu_memory_utilization=0.85). Use SamplingParams(temperature=0, max_tokens=512, stop=['

Question:', '

Problem:']). Process all 2,400 instances in batches. Save raw outputs to results/raw/phi35_seed42.jsonl. Parse answers and save to results/parsed/phi35_seed42.json. Log inference time.
steps.step2: Evaluate Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on seed_42 dataset. Same vLLM configuration. Apply the Llama chat template. Save raw outputs to results/raw/llama31_8b_seed42.jsonl. Parse and save to results/parsed/llama31_8b_seed42.json.
steps.step3: Evaluate Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct on seed_42 dataset. Same vLLM configuration. Apply Qwen chat template. Save raw outputs to results/raw/qwen25_7b_seed42.jsonl. Parse and save to results/parsed/qwen25_7b_seed42.json.
steps.step4: Evaluate Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct-AWQ (4-bit quantized) on seed_42 dataset. Load with vLLM using quantization='awq'. This tests scaling to a larger model within memory constraints. Save to results/raw/qwen25_32b_seed42.jsonl and results/parsed/qwen25_32b_seed42.json.
steps.step5: For cross-seed variance estimation: evaluate Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on seed_123 and seed_456 datasets. Save to results/raw/llama31_8b_seed{123,456}.jsonl and parsed equivalents.

title: Evaluate reasoning-optimized model
description: Evaluate the reasoning-optimized model (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B) which was trained with reinforcement learning on reasoning tasks. This is the key comparison: does reasoning-focused training reduce the Directional Reasoning Gap?

steps.step1: Evaluate DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on seed_42 dataset using vLLM. Use max_tokens=1024 (R1 models produce longer chain-of-thought outputs). Save raw outputs to results/raw/deepseek_r1_7b_seed42.jsonl. Parse final answers (the R1 model wraps its answer in <answer> tags or after 'ANSWER:') and save to results/parsed/deepseek_r1_7b_seed42.json.
steps.step2: For cross-seed variance: evaluate DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on seed_123 and seed_456 datasets. Save to results/raw/deepseek_r1_7b_seed{123,456}.jsonl and parsed equivalents.
steps.step3: Compare DRG between DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and size-matched standard models (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). Test hypothesis: reasoning-optimized models have significantly smaller DRG. Use paired comparison on the same problem instances.

title: Ablation 1: Effect of Chain-of-Thought prompting on DRG
description: Test whether adding a 'think step by step' instruction reduces the directional reasoning gap. This ablates the prompt format — if CoT reduces DRG, it suggests the gap is partly due to the model not spontaneously applying backward reasoning strategies.

steps.step1: Re-evaluate Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on seed_42 using the CoT prompt template (from prompts.py). Use max_tokens=1024 to accommodate longer reasoning chains. Save to results/raw/llama31_8b_cot_seed42.jsonl.
steps.step2: Re-evaluate DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on seed_42 using the CoT prompt template. Save to results/raw/deepseek_r1_7b_cot_seed42.jsonl.
steps.step3: Parse CoT outputs and compare: (a) DRG with vs. without CoT for each model, (b) whether CoT disproportionately helps backward problems, (c) whether CoT + reasoning-optimized model further reduces DRG. Save comparison to results/aggregated/cot_ablation.json.

title: Ablation 2: DRG across difficulty levels
description: Analyze how the Directional Reasoning Gap scales with problem difficulty within each domain. This tests the hypothesis that DRG amplifies with complexity.

steps.step1: For each model evaluated on seed_42, compute DRG broken down by domain AND difficulty level (4 domains × 3 difficulty levels = 12 cells per model). Save to results/aggregated/drg_by_difficulty.json.
steps.step2: Test the trend: within each domain, does DRG(difficulty_3) > DRG(difficulty_2) > DRG(difficulty_1)? Use a one-sided paired t-test (or Wilcoxon signed-rank test if non-normal) across models. Report p-values.
steps.step3: Compute the difficulty amplification factor: DRG(hard) / DRG(easy) for each domain. A factor > 1 supports the hypothesis.

title: Ablation 3: Domain-specific DRG analysis
description: Analyze which reasoning domains exhibit the largest directional asymmetry and whether cross-domain DRG correlations exist.

steps.step1: Compute per-domain DRG for each model. Rank domains by average DRG across all models. Test whether the ranking matches our hypothesis: logic > relational > function > arithmetic.
steps.step2: Compute cross-domain DRG correlation: for each pair of domains, compute Pearson correlation of per-model DRG values. If models symmetric in one domain tend to be symmetric in others, this suggests a shared underlying ability.
steps.step3: Error analysis: for each domain, categorize backward-reasoning errors into types. Logic: failure to apply contraposition, incorrect negation propagation, distractor confusion. Arithmetic: incorrect inverse operation, wrong operand identification. Relational: incorrect traversal direction, entity confusion. Function: inability to invert, incorrect compositional inversion. Sample 50 errors per domain from the worst-performing model and manually categorize. Save to results/aggregated/error_analysis.json.

title: Statistical testing and success criteria evaluation
description: Apply rigorous statistical tests to determine whether observed DRGs are significant and evaluate all success criteria from the proposal.

steps.step1: For each model × domain combination, test whether DRG > 0 using a one-sided McNemar's test (comparing paired forward vs. backward accuracy on the same problem instances). Apply Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons (5 models × 4 domains = 20 tests). Report corrected p-values.
steps.step2: Test primary success criterion 1: 'DRG > 5pp in at least 3/4 domains across majority of models'. Count how many model-domain pairs have DRG > 5pp with p < 0.05.
steps.step3: Test primary success criterion 2: 'DRG increases with difficulty in at least 2 domains'. Use the results from Ablation 2.
steps.step4: Test primary success criterion 3: 'Benchmark reveals meaningful differences between model families'. Compute effect size (Cohen's d) for DRG between standard vs. reasoning-optimized models.
steps.step5: Test secondary criterion: 'Consistency rate < min(FA, BA)'. For each model × domain, compute consistency rate and compare to min(FA, BA). If CR < min(FA, BA), it means the model fails different instances in each direction (not just uniformly worse).
steps.step6: Cross-seed stability: using the 2 models evaluated on all 3 seeds, compute the standard deviation of DRG across seeds for each domain. Report mean ± std. If std < 3pp, the benchmark is stable.
steps.step7: Save all statistical results to results/aggregated/statistical_tests.json. Create a summary table with all metrics for the paper.

title: Generate paper figures and tables
description: Create publication-quality figures and tables summarizing all results. Target: 5-7 figures for the paper.

steps.step1: Figure 1 — Overview heatmap: Create a heatmap (models × domains) showing DRG values. Color scale: green (low DRG) to red (high DRG). Annotate each cell with the DRG value. Include a separate panel or row for CoT results. Save as figures/fig1_drg_heatmap.pdf and .png.
steps.step2: Figure 2 — Forward vs. Backward accuracy scatter: For each model × domain, plot a scatter point with FA on x-axis and BA on y-axis. The diagonal line represents DRG=0 (perfect symmetry). Points below the diagonal indicate forward bias. Color by model, shape by domain. Save as figures/fig2_forward_vs_backward.pdf.
steps.step3: Figure 3 — DRG vs. difficulty: Grouped bar chart. X-axis: difficulty levels (1, 2, 3). Y-axis: DRG. Grouped by domain, with error bars from cross-seed variance. One panel per domain (2×2 subplot). Save as figures/fig3_drg_by_difficulty.pdf.
steps.step4: Figure 4 — Standard vs. reasoning-optimized comparison: Side-by-side bar chart comparing DRG of Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct vs. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (same architecture family, different training) across all 4 domains. Include error bars. Save as figures/fig4_standard_vs_reasoning.pdf.
steps.step5: Figure 5 — CoT ablation: Bar chart showing DRG with and without CoT prompting for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and DeepSeek-R1-7B, broken down by domain. Save as figures/fig5_cot_ablation.pdf.
steps.step6: Figure 6 — Consistency analysis: Stacked bar chart per model showing: (a) both correct, (b) forward only correct, (c) backward only correct, (d) both wrong. Broken down by domain. Save as figures/fig6_consistency.pdf.
steps.step7: Figure 7 — Error type distribution: Pie charts or horizontal bar charts showing the distribution of backward error types per domain (from error analysis). Save as figures/fig7_error_types.pdf.
steps.step8: Table 1 — Main results table: LaTeX-formatted table with columns: Model, Domain, FA, BA, DRG, CR, p-value. Bold the largest DRG per domain. Save as figures/table1_main_results.tex.
steps.step9: Table 2 — Cross-seed stability: Table showing DRG mean ± std across 3 seeds for the 2 models evaluated on all seeds. Save as figures/table2_seed_stability.tex.

title: Runtime estimation and execution schedule
description: Estimate total runtime and plan sequential execution order to fit within the 8-hour budget on 1x A6000 GPU.

steps.step1: Estimated runtime breakdown: (a) Environment setup + model download: 30 min. (b) Dataset generation (3 seeds, all 4 domains): 20 min (CPU-only). (c) Simple baselines (random + heuristic): 5 min (CPU-only). (d) Model inference — Phi-3.5-mini (3.8B): ~15 min for 2,400 instances. (e) Llama-3.1-8B: ~25 min × 3 seeds = 75 min. (f) Qwen2.5-7B: ~25 min. (g) DeepSeek-R1-7B: ~35 min × 3 seeds = 105 min (longer due to CoT output). (h) Qwen2.5-32B-AWQ: ~50 min. (i) CoT ablation (2 models): ~60 min. (j) Analysis + statistical tests: 15 min. (k) Visualization: 20 min. TOTAL ESTIMATE: ~7 hours.
steps.step2: Execution order (sequential on 1 GPU): 1. Setup → 2. Data generation → 3. Simple baselines → 4. Phi-3.5-mini → 5. Llama-3.1-8B (seed_42) → 6. Qwen2.5-7B → 7. DeepSeek-R1-7B (seed_42) → 8. Qwen2.5-32B-AWQ → 9. Llama-3.1-8B (seeds 123, 456) → 10. DeepSeek-R1-7B (seeds 123, 456) → 11. CoT ablation → 12. Analysis → 13. Visualization. Note: data generation and simple baselines are CPU-only and can overlap with model downloads.
steps.step3: Contingency: if inference runs slower than estimated, drop Phi-3.5-mini (least informative model) and/or reduce CoT ablation to 1 model. If running ahead of schedule, add Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 as an additional baseline.
